title: State and Memory
description: The Memory interface saves and restores deployment state between runs.
why_persistence_body: CReact apps describe infrastructure and processes. When the app stops and restarts, you need to know what was already deployed, otherwise every restart re-creates everything from scratch. The <0>Memory</0> interface solves this by saving and restoring deployment state.
memory_interface_intro: A <0>Memory</0> implementation supplies two methods, <1>getState</1> and <2>saveState</2>. The example <3>FileMemory</3> reads and writes one JSON file per stack, and CReact never ships a built-in backend so you choose the storage.
using_memory_intro: The entry point constructs a <0>FileMemory</0> and hands it to <1>render()</1> along with the stack name. This function is all a CReact app needs to boot.
tip_stack_name: The third argument to <0>render()</0> is the stack name. It names the state file, so distinct stack names keep independent state stores side by side.
use_async_output_intro: <0>useAsyncOutput</0> owns the durable outputs of a resource. The counter handler seeds its count from <1>setOutputs(prev => ...)</1>, so a restart resumes from the last saved value instead of zero.
what_gets_persisted_body: Only values passed to <0>setOutputs()</0> are persisted. Regular signals, effects, and local variables are <1>not</1> saved, so they reinitialize on each run.
info_gitignore: Add the state directory such as <0>.state/</0> to your <1>.gitignore</1>.
durable_counter_link: See Memory, setOutputs, and crash recovery working end to end in the <0>durable-counter walkthrough</0>.
